schools.summary: Paul departed Athens and came to Corinth — moving from the philosophic city to the wealthy, dissolute commercial center of Achaia (Henry), where a great church would be founded; the gospel finding more fruit among the dissolute Corinthians than the proud Athenian philosophers.

themes: Paul departed from Athens, and came to Corinth
from the philosophic to the commercial city
the founding of the Corinthian church

schools.summary: Paul found Aquila and Priscilla, expelled from Rome by Claudius's edict — which Suetonius attributes to tumults under 'Chrestus,' possibly a garbled reference to Christ and the disturbances over the gospel (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson); they became Paul's hosts and fellow-workers, Priscilla often named first (Robertson).
Calvin: the providence — Aquila's exile, a calamity, became the means of his meeting Paul; God often provides better through affliction.

themes: a certain Jew named Aquila with his wife Priscilla
Claudius had commanded all Jews to depart from Rome

schools.summary: Paul, of the same trade (tentmaking), lodged and worked with Aquila — not ashamed of honest labor (Barnes), following the Jewish custom of every man learning a trade (Robertson, Wesley, the rabbinic principle that not teaching a son a trade is teaching him to steal).
Calvin: Paul worked to keep the gospel free from reproach, refusing support lest the false apostles slander him (1 Corinthians 9:12, 15).

themes: he abode with them, and wrought

schools.summary: Paul reasoned in the synagogue every sabbath, persuading Jews and Greeks — discoursing on Jesus as Messiah from the Scriptures (Clarke), trying to convince them (the conative imperfect, Robertson); his diligence in laboring by day and preaching on the sabbath (Gill).

themes: he reasoned in the synagogue every sabbath
persuaded the Jews and the Greeks

schools.summary: When Silas and Timothy came, Paul was constrained to testify Jesus is the Christ; the better MSS read 'constrained by the word' (Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson) rather than 'pressed in the spirit' (TR); the arrival with Macedonian gifts (2 Corinthians 11:9) freed Paul from tentmaking to preach with fresh consecration (Robertson).

debates.question: Does the text read 'pressed in the spirit' or 'constrained by the word'?

sides.position: 'Constrained by the word' — the better-attested reading
'Pressed in the spirit' — the Textus Receptus reading

themes: Silas and Timotheus were come from Macedonia
pressed in the spirit / constrained by the word

schools.summary: When the Jews opposed and blasphemed, Paul shook his raiment (a gesture of detestation, like Nehemiah 5:13) and declared 'your blood be upon your own heads; I am clean' — the watchman's disclaimer (Ezekiel 33, Ellicott, Robertson); turning to the Gentiles locally, giving up the Corinthian synagogue, not all Jews (Ellicott, as 13:46).
Calvin: God is more displeased with contempt of his word than any wickedness; Paul, like Ezekiel's watchman, having warned, is clean of their blood, and turns to the Gentiles.

themes: your blood be upon your own heads; I am clean

schools.summary: Paul moved his preaching to the house of Justus, a God-fearer, adjoining the synagogue — changing his place of preaching, not his lodging (still with Aquila, Henry); the house next door chosen in charity, that Paul might remain near the Jews, ready to receive any who would hear (Henry).

themes: entered into a certain man's house, named Justus

schools.summary: Crispus, the synagogue ruler, believed with all his house — a signal triumph Paul honored by baptizing him himself (1 Corinthians 1:14, Barnes) — and many Corinthians believed and were baptized; the harvest in dissolute Corinth displaying that a church founded there can be founded anywhere (Barnes).

themes: Crispus, the chief ruler of the synagogue, believed
many of the Corinthians believed and were baptized

schools.summary: The Lord spoke to Paul in a vision — 'be not afraid, but speak' — meeting his discouragement (the fear and trembling he confesses, 1 Corinthians 2:3, Barnes); one of several visions at the crises of Paul's life (Ellicott), his discouragement like Elijah's and Jeremiah's.
Calvin: nothing more contrary to the free preaching of the gospel than a faint heart (2 Timothy 1:7); the command charges Paul to speak plainly and fully, overcoming fear.

themes: the Lord spake to Paul in the night by a vision

schools.summary: The Lord promised 'I am with thee' (Matthew 28:20 personally applied, Ellicott) and protection, 'for I have much people in this city'; Barnes: God's certain purpose to save, the ground of evangelistic hope — had God no purpose to save, the minister could have no hope.

Calvin (with Wesley): the 'much people' are the foreknown elect, not yet converted but written in the book of life (John 17:6), whom God would gather through Paul's preaching — God taking them as his people in respect of their future faith.

debates.question: Who are the 'much people' God has in Corinth?

sides.position: The foreknown elect, not yet converted but written in the book of life, certain to be gathered

themes: I have much people in this city

schools.summary: Paul continued eighteen months at Corinth, teaching the word — an unusually long stay (Barnes), caused by his success and the need to establish the church firmly; the work extending beyond the city to Cenchrea and all Achaia (Ellicott, Robertson).
Calvin: the labor of founding a church is hard and sustained — the great workman spent so long laying the foundation of one church only.

themes: he continued there a year and six months
the patient founding of a church

schools.summary: When Gallio (Seneca's brother, a mild and beloved man, Gill) was proconsul of Achaia, the Jews made a concerted assault on Paul, bringing him to the judgment seat; Gallio's proconsulship a key chronological anchor for Paul's life (about A.D. 51-52).

themes: when Gallio was the deputy of Achaia
the Jews made insurrection against Paul

schools.summary: The Jews charged Paul with worship 'contrary to the law' — which must mean Roman law (Ellicott, Robertson): the Jews claimed Judaism was a licensed religion and accused Paul of preaching a new, unlicensed one; the legal strategy to show Paul violated Roman regulations on converts (Robertson).

debates.question: Which law did the Jews mean — Roman or Mosaic?

sides.position: Roman law — claiming Paul preached an unlicensed religion (not a religio licita)
Possibly both, or the law of Moses

themes: this fellow persuadeth men to worship contrary to the law

schools.summary: Before Paul could begin his defense (the formal apologia, Ellicott), Gallio interrupted — distinguishing real crimes ('wrong or wicked lewdness,' legal wrong or fraud, Robertson, Ellicott), which he would judge, from the religious question, which he would not; the conditional implying the Jews had no legal case (Robertson).

themes: when Paul was about to open his mouth
if it were a matter of wrong or wicked lewdness

schools.summary: Gallio refused to judge 'a question of words and names, and of your law' — perceiving it was Jewish, not Roman law (Ellicott); an absolute refusal to allow a religious question in a Roman court (Robertson); the decision, treating Christianity as a form of Judaism, in effect opening the door for Paul's preaching across the empire (Robertson); Clarke praising the principle of religious liberty.

themes: a question of words and names, and of your law
look ye to it; for I will be no judge
the refusal to judge religious questions
the legal toleration of the gospel

schools.summary: Gallio drove the Jews from the judgment seat — an authoritative dismissal, not violent but magisterial (Barnes, Clarke), the lictors clearing the court (Ellicott); the Jews stunned by the sudden blow from the mild proconsul (Robertson), their assault utterly failed.

themes: he drave them from the judgment seat
the failure of the Jewish assault

schools.summary: The crowd beat Sosthenes (likely Crispus's successor, probably later a Christian, 1 Corinthians 1:1); 'Gallio cared for none of those things' strongly defended against the charge of religious indifference — meaning only that he would not meddle in a Jewish quarrel outside his province, not the indifference of a skeptic (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson).
Calvin: the Spirit condemns Gallio's carelessness in not protecting an unjustly punished man — the magistrate's duty to aid the oppressed, his indifference to Sosthenes's beating a fault.

debates.question: Does 'Gallio cared for none of those things' show blameworthy religious indifference?

sides.position: No — it means only that he would not meddle in a Jewish quarrel outside his jurisdiction; the common reading misuses the text
It marks a blameworthy carelessness — a magistrate ought to protect the oppressed

themes: all the Greeks took Sosthenes and beat him
Gallio cared for none of those things

schools.summary: Paul sailed for Syria with Priscilla and Aquila, having shorn his head at Cenchrea for a vow; the Greek ambiguous on whose vow (Paul's or Aquila's, Henry), most referring it to Paul; Ellicott justifies it as Paul being 'all things to all men' (1 Corinthians 9:20), testifying he did not despise the Law, the shearing guarding against effeminate long hair.
Chrysostom: the Law breaking up yet conscience still binding — a Jewish custom to shear the head for a vow; Paul not yet withdrawing the Jewish believers from their custom.

debates.question: Whose vow was it, and was Paul right to keep a Jewish vow?

sides.position: The Greek is ambiguous (Paul's or Aquila's); if Paul's, a lawful accommodation, being 'all things to all men' without compromising the gospel

themes: having shorn his head in Cenchrea, for he had a vow

schools.summary: Paul came to Ephesus (the great capital of Asia, home of Diana's worship, Robertson, earlier forbidden to him, 16:6); he left Aquila and Priscilla there (that they might teach, Chrysostom) and reasoned in the synagogue — a brief visit preparing for the later three-year ministry (ch. 19).

themes: he came to Ephesus, and left them there

schools.summary: The Ephesians asked Paul to stay longer, but he consented not — their eagerness a hopeful sign of the great receptivity Paul found at Ephesus (Ellicott), foreshadowing the fruitful three-year ministry to come; Paul declining, determined to reach Jerusalem for the feast (v.21).

themes: they desired him to tarry longer

schools.summary: Paul bade the Ephesians farewell, determined to keep the feast at Jerusalem (to win his countrymen, Barnes, Wesley — the clause textually doubtful), promising to return 'if God will' — the pious submission of his plans to God's will (Ellicott, Robertson, James 4:15); fulfilling the promise at 19:1.

themes: I must keep this feast that cometh in Jerusalem
I will return again unto you, if God will

schools.summary: Paul landed at Caesarea, went up (to Jerusalem, the customary idiom, Clarke, Robertson) and saluted the church (his fourth post-conversion visit), then went down to Antioch — closing the second missionary journey; the Jerusalem visit brief (Calvin), perhaps completing the vow in the temple (Ellicott).

themes: landed at Caesarea, and gone up, and saluted the church
the close of the second journey

schools.summary: Paul began the third journey, going through Galatia and Phrygia 'in order' (systematically, Clarke), strengthening all the disciples — beginning alone without Barnabas or Silas (Robertson), revisiting his churches; the pastoral purpose to confirm them (as 15:41), the visit reflected in Galatians before the Judaizers' damage (Ellicott).

themes: went over all the country of Galatia and Phrygia in order

schools.summary: Apollos, a Jew of Alexandria, eloquent and mighty in the Scriptures, came to Ephesus — combining the rhetorical training of Alexandria (home of Philo) with deep knowledge of the Old Testament (Henry); a remarkable figure who would labor at Corinth (1 Corinthians 1-3).

themes: a certain Jew named Apollos, born at Alexandria
an eloquent man, and mighty in the scriptures
learned men rising in the church

schools.summary: Apollos was instructed (catechized) in the way of the Lord, fervent in spirit (his own zealous spirit, Romans 12:11, Ellicott, Robertson), teaching diligently, yet 'knowing only the baptism of John' — accurate in what he knew, but his knowledge stopping short of the Cross, Resurrection, and Pentecost (Robertson).
Calvin: zeal joined to knowledge — doctrine without zeal is cold, but zeal must be governed by knowledge of the Scripture; Apollos's fervor a gift of the Spirit.

themes: instructed in the way of the Lord
knowing only the baptism of John

schools.summary: Apollos preached boldly, and Aquila and Priscilla took him aside and expounded the way more perfectly — a gentle, delicate correction of a gifted preacher (Robertson); Apollos, the eloquent scholar, humbly received instruction from a Christian woman and her husband (Clarke).
Calvin: women were not as ignorant as the Papists claim — yet Priscilla taught privately at home, not overthrowing the order of public teaching prescribed by God and nature.

debates.question: How does Priscilla's teaching of Apollos bear on women teaching?

sides.position: It shows women's knowledge of the word, but was private instruction at home, not public office — not overthrowing church order

themes: he began to speak boldly in the synagogue
Aquila and Priscilla expounded the way of God more perfectly

schools.summary: Apollos, going to Achaia (Corinth), was commended by the Ephesian brethren in a letter — the first instance of a 'letter of commendation' (2 Corinthians 3:1, Ellicott) — and helped the believers much, strengthening them against the Jews (Barnes); 'through grace' going with 'helped' or 'believed,' both true (Robertson).

themes: the brethren wrote, exhorting the disciples to receive him
helped them much which had believed through grace

schools.summary: Apollos mightily and publicly confuted the Jews, showing by the Scriptures that Jesus is the Christ — his reasoning strong, arguments weighty, scriptural proofs irresistible (Gill, Barnes), bearing down all opposition; like Paul, proving from the Old Testament that Jesus is the Messiah.

themes: he mightily convinced the Jews, and that publicly
shewing by the scriptures that Jesus was Christ
